ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for gyudon with daikon and ponzu sauce. FOODON_00002572 (beef rice bowl food product) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Beef dishes) is the closest defensible parent. No specific gyudon or gyudon-with-daikon code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for gyudon or this specific combo dish. SNOMED CT does not cover prepared Japanese rice bowl dishes at this level of specificity.

allergens: fish (ponzu contains citrus and typically bonito/fish-based dashi)

serving.description: 1 combo set (gyudon bowl + half-boiled egg + miso soup)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard combo serving consists of approximately 550 g total, comprising a gyudon rice bowl with daikon topping (approximately 400 g), one half-boiled egg (approximately 50 g), and a bowl of miso soup (approximately 100 g).

nutrient_source.title: Gyudon (beef rice bowl) — composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked white rice, simmered beef, daikon radish, egg, and miso soup; cross-referenced with NCC Food and Nutrient Database generic beef rice bowl category
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central; NCC Food and Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for gyudon with diced and grated daikon and ponzu sauce as a complete dish. Nutrient values are estimated by component-level composition: cooked short-grain white rice (~200 g per serving, USDA FDC #168878), simmered beef slices (~100 g, USDA FDC #174032), daikon radish raw (~50 g, USDA FDC #169231), half-boiled egg (~50 g, USDA FDC #173424), miso soup (~100 g, USDA FDC #172457), ponzu sauce (~20 g, USDA FDC #2103689). Values are expressed per 100 g of the combined dish. Confidence is medium due to estimation from components.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (Japanese short-grain, cooked) GI approximately 72; gyudon composite estimated from rice-dominant GI
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for gyudon with daikon and ponzu sauce as a complete dish. GI is estimated at 72 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being Japanese short-grain white rice, which has a published GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021). Protein and fat from beef and egg moderately attenuate postprandial glucose response; daikon and ponzu contribute minimal carbohydrate. The composite GI is estimated conservatively at 72 (high category). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (72 × 85.25 g available carbohydrate per 550 g serving) / 100 = approximately 61.4, rounded to 61.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce, miso soup, and ponzu sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content (approximately 310 mg per 100 g, equating to roughly 1,705 mg sodium per 550 g serving), which approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; requesting reduced-sodium soy sauce and omitting or reducing miso soup can meaningfully lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index from white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish with caution given its high glycemic index (estimated GI 72) and high glycemic load per serving (approximately 61), which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; portion reduction, substitution of white rice with lower-GI alternatives such as brown rice or cauliflower rice, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables are recommended strategies.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from beef and elevated sodium from condiments
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of this dish's moderate saturated fat content (approximately 1.1 g per 100 g from beef) and high sodium load from soy sauce, miso, and ponzu; choosing leaner beef cuts, reducing sauce quantities, and limiting serving frequency to occasional consumption is advisable to support cardiovascular health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Diced and Grated Daikon Gyudon with Ponzu Sauce Combo is a Japanese beef rice bowl dish (gyudon) distinguished by a dual-texture daikon radish topping — both finely diced cubes and freshly grated daikon (daikon oroshi) — drizzled with ponzu sauce, a citrus-soy condiment that imparts a bright, tangy, and umami-rich flavour profile. The gyudon base consists of thinly sliced beef simmered in a sweet-savoury dashi broth seasoned with soy sauce, mirin, and sake, served over a bowl of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice. The ponzu sauce adds a refreshing citrus acidity that cuts through the richness of the beef, while the daikon provides a cooling, mildly peppery crunch and digestive enzyme content. The combo set is served with a half-boiled egg (or an alternative side with top-up) and a bowl of miso soup or a beverage. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-protein, high-carbohydrate meal with significant sodium from the soy-based condiments and miso soup. The glycemic index is high (estimated GI 72) due to the white rice base, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is a popular fast-casual Japanese meal widely available in Singapore through chains such as Sukiya. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those requiring halal certification without specific confirmation from the restaurant. Key allergens include soy, wheat, egg, and fish (from ponzu and dashi).
common_user_queries: What is in a gyudon combo?
What does daikon taste like in gyudon?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific dish (gyudon with diced and grated daikon and ponzu sauce as a complete combo). Nutrient values are estimated by component-level composition using USDA FoodData Central entries for each major ingredient. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source (Japanese short-grain white rice, GI 72) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are individually well-supported and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are per 100 g of the complete combo dish (gyudon bowl + half-boiled egg + miso soup, total ~550 g serving). Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries; no single approved-source entry covers this exact dish. 2. Sodium content is notably high due to soy sauce, miso soup, and ponzu sauce; actual sodium may vary by restaurant preparation. 3. GI is estimated at 72 (high) based on Japanese short-grain white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source; the protein and fat from beef and egg provide modest attenuation of postprandial glucose response. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FDC component data and are not directly reported from a single source entry; these values carry medium uncertainty. 5. Halal status is not confirmed; Sukiya Singapore's halal certification status should be verified independently. 6. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON included irrelevant tags (Japanese Curry, Grilled Chicken Skewers, Fried Chicken) that appear to be platform-level category tags rather than dish-specific descriptors; these have been disregarded. 7. Price is SGD 9.00 as reported by field survey data (2 restaurants: Sukiya - Funan and Funan - Mix & Match).
